# Meeting Summary for Community WG

**NOTICE**: This summary was auto-generated by Zoom's "AI". AI-generated
content may be inaccurate or misleading. Always check for accuracy. If in
doubt, please consult the meeting recording at
https://youtube.com/@GraphQLFoundation/playlists

- Meeting start: 2026-06-11T14:57:14Z
- Meeting end: 2026-06-11T16:00:59Z
- Summary start: 2026-06-11T14:57:14Z
- Summary end: 2026-06-11T16:00:57Z

The GraphQL Community Working Group meeting focused on three main topics: consolidating community communication channels, improving the community website, and developing a content management hub. Jean proposed making Discord the main hub for community communication to address accessibility issues with Slack, particularly for external collaborators like Airbnb representatives who cannot access corporate Slack accounts. The group discussed updating the community website's landing page and team sections to better display foundation members and board representatives as individuals rather than company names. The third major topic involved developing a community content hub (dubbed "Gateloom") that would integrate with GitHub and Discord for content review and management, starting with ambassador content and potentially expanding to include social media posts and community resources. The team also reviewed a new tagline for GraphQL - "the API language for humans and agents" - which received positive feedback from the group, particularly from Salomé who provided marketing perspective on the copy and suggested animated elements for the website.

## Next Steps

- Jean: Create and refine the detailed spec/PRD for the initial alpha version of the community hub (Gateloom), focusing on authentication, roles, Typefully integration, and basic workflows for ambassadors
- Jean: Update and maintain the GitHub issue for the community page and team page, and continue discussion on Discord and GitHub
- Salomé: Help draft and review text/content for the new community page and contribute to defining the community hub requirements
- Salomé: Join the Discord server and engage in the Community Working Group Discord channel
- Jean: Coordinate with Jem (and possibly others) on Discord role management and ambassador role setup
- Martin: Share Buffer.com credentials with Jean and investigate Buffer GraphQL API for potential integration
- Martin: Reach out to Buffer.com to explore the possibility of increased account access or support for the GraphQL community
- Jean: Request Viaduct stickers from HQ in San Francisco and coordinate sending them to Riggin in Bangalore and Benjie in London for upcoming GraphQL Day events
- Salomé: Review and suggest new copy/bullet points for the GraphQL website tagline and hero section, including AI-era messaging
- Jean: Add emphasis and review of code of conduct and EasyCLA process for community hub onboarding, referencing prior GitHub issues
- Jean: Continue organizing and maturing the proposal for a future GraphQL-focused software conference (target 2027), and bring to foundation meeting when ready
- Salomé: Think about and propose bullet points and copy suggestions for the website, especially in relation to the new tagline and AI focus
- Jean: Update the GitHub issue regarding the team page to clarify who is working on different areas (e.g., GraphiQL, GraphQL JS, socials, YouTube, ambassadors) and foundation/board member disclosure
- All: Continue collaboration and updates on Discord and GitHub until the next meeting

## Summary

### Amsterdam GraphQL Presentation Discussion

The group discussed their recent meeting in Amsterdam where Martin presented GraphQL topics to an audience wearing pink t-shirts. Martin reported that the format worked well with five talks covering topics including introspection, community introductions, and AppSync. The meeting was about to begin with standard disclaimers regarding code of conduct and recording for YouTube publication.

### Discord as Main Community Hub

Jean proposed making Discord the main hub for community communication to address accessibility issues with current platforms like Slack, particularly for external participants from companies with strict vendor access policies. The team discussed challenges with multiple communication channels and agreed that Discord offers better accessibility and cost-effectiveness than other options like creating official Slack accounts. Jean suggested standardizing community channels and moving the official Discord link to a more prominent location on the website to reinforce it as the primary community platform.

### Website Navigation and Team Pages

Jean proposed creating a slash community page and improving the website's navigation for newcomers. The team discussed updating the team page to clearly display individual foundation members and board members, with Martin suggesting a broader "slash team" page that includes all contributors and ambassadors. Jean agreed to create a GitHub issue for the team page updates and will work on the slash community landing page, with Martin advising to work in the open and ping people regularly for feedback.

### Community Content Hub Development

The team discussed developing a Community Content Hub at hub.graphql.org that would integrate with GitHub and Discord for managing GraphQL community content and roles. Jean presented the basic concept which includes authentication for ambassadors, content review processes, and potential integration with Typefully and Buffer's new GraphQL API. The group agreed to move forward with developing the hub, with Salomé offering to help with content writing and Jean planning to create a more specific specification for the initial alpha version. The team also reviewed a new tagline "GraphQL: The API Language for Humans and Agents" that Martin had proposed for the website, which Salomé supported and agreed to help refine the supporting bullet points for.
